common_name: Corbaci Pepper (Turkish Long Sweet Frying Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting after last frost; Corbaci is a Turkish heirloom sweet frying pepper producing long, slender fruits (8–12 inches) that taper to a point. The name 'Corbaci' derives from a Turkish rank (commander) and reflects the regional importance of this pepper type in Turkish cuisine. Fruits are harvested green (lime green) for fresh eating and frying, or left to ripen fully to red; both stages are sweet with no heat. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer production and again August–September for fall harvest. Corbaci is extremely productive and performs well in warm Gulf Coast conditions, where its heat tolerance gives it an advantage over thicker-walled sweet bell types that suffer in high summer temperatures.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–85°F; transplant after last frost to full sun and harvest green at 8–10 inches or allow to ripen red. Corbaci Pepper is a long-standing Turkish heirloom sweet frying pepper that has gained significant popularity among American specialty growers and seed-savers for its combination of outstanding productivity, heat tolerance, and distinctive flavor profile. Unlike standard bell peppers, which develop thick walls that can flag in extreme heat, Corbaci's thin-walled, elongated fruits tolerate zone 9 summer temperatures without developing bitter off-flavors or collapsing at the blossom end — a common issue with large-fruited sweet peppers in the Deep South. The fruits ripen from lime green through yellow to vivid red; all stages are usable, but the fully red fruit develops a richer, slightly sweeter flavor with a soft, tender texture ideal for roasting, grilling, or raw eating. Corbaci is particularly well-suited to frying in olive oil (a classic Turkish preparation called 'körbacı biber tava'), where the thin walls soften quickly and the skin blisters attractively without the need for extended cooking time. As an open-pollinated heirloom, seeds can be saved true-to-type each season. The plants are vigorous and upright with good leaf cover that provides some protection to the pendant fruits in intense afternoon sun. Corbaci performs best with consistent moisture but tolerates brief drought better than bell types due to its smaller fruit size and lower water demand per fruit.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Corbaci Pepper heirloom variety documentation and Turkish origin notes
Pepper Joe's and Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Corbaci variety trial notes and culinary description
LSU AgCenter Extension — Sweet Pepper Variety Performance in Louisiana Gulf Coast Conditions

common_name: Alma Paprika Pepper (Hungarian Round Paprika for Drying and Grinding)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Alma Paprika is a Hungarian heirloom producing small round fruits (1.5–2 inches diameter) that ripen from creamy white through yellow to vivid red. The round shape and thick but not waxy flesh make it ideal for drying and grinding into genuine sweet Hungarian paprika. In zone 9, transplant March–April; the compact plant habit (18–24 inches) makes it suitable for containers. Fruits are very mild (500–1,500 SHU) with a rich, sweet paprika flavor that develops fully when dried. Unlike many commercially produced paprika peppers that are harvested by machine and dried at high temperatures, home-dried Alma Paprika can be slow-dried at low heat to preserve volatile aromatics.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–85°F; transplant after last frost and harvest when fully red for drying. Alma Paprika Pepper is a Hungarian heirloom variety prized by home paprika makers for its compact, round fruits that dry easily and produce an aromatic, sweet paprika powder with far more complexity than commercially available dried paprika. The variety belongs to the traditional Hungarian paprika pepper classifications — distinguished from the longer 'szegedi' or 'kalocsal' types by its apple-like round shape — and is named 'Alma' (Hungarian for 'apple') in direct reference to this distinctive fruit form. The flavor progression through ripening stages is one of this variety's most interesting characteristics: at the creamy-white stage the fruit is mildly sweet and slightly starchy, similar to a mild Italian frying pepper; at yellow it develops a fruitier, slightly citrusy edge; at full red ripeness the paprika sweetness is fully expressed with a rich, slightly smoky underlying note. Gardeners can use Alma Paprika at any ripe stage for fresh eating — it performs well in salads, stuffed preparations, and light sautés — but its primary use is drying. Halved and laid cut-side-down on screens in warm sun or placed in a food dehydrator at 120–130°F, the fruits dry thoroughly within 3–5 days and grind to a vivid orange-red powder. Compact plant size makes Alma Paprika one of the most productive paprika types per square foot of garden space.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Alma Paprika Hungarian heirloom pepper variety documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Alma Paprika variety notes and drying instructions
University of New Mexico Chile Pepper Institute — Hungarian Paprika Classification and Home Production

common_name: Wapsipinicon Peach Tomato (Iowa Heirloom Peach-Yellow Sweet Cherry Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Wapsipinicon Peach is an indeterminate open-pollinated cherry tomato producing 1–1.5-inch round fruits with a distinctive pale peach-yellow color and a slightly fuzzy-looking surface texture that inspired the 'peach' descriptor. Named for the Wapsipinicon River in northeastern Iowa where it originated, this variety has been maintained by seed-savers as an example of mid-American heirloom tomato diversity. In zone 9, transplant March–April and stake or cage to 6 feet; indeterminate growth continues until frost. The pale coloration indicates low lycopene (the pigment responsible for red color) and high carotenoid content of a different type, contributing to the notably sweet, low-acid flavor profile that has made this variety a favorite among cherry tomato enthusiasts seeking flavor over color.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–85°F; transplant after last frost and stake or cage to 6 feet. Harvest when fruits yield gently to pressure and separate easily from the calyx. Wapsipinicon Peach Tomato is an Iowa heritage cherry tomato distinguished by its pale peach-yellow coloration and the exceptionally sweet, low-acid flavor that develops as fruits ripen fully. The name refers to both the peach-like appearance of the ripe fruit (the pale skin with a subtle blushed tinge on the sunny side closely resembles a small yellow peach) and the Wapsipinicon River region of northeastern Iowa where the variety was grown and saved by farm families through the twentieth century. The variety reached national seed-saver networks through the Seed Savers Exchange network and has been grown in trial gardens across the country where it consistently earns high marks for flavor complexity — a balance of sweetness with sufficient acid to provide depth, rather than the flat sweetness of some low-acid cherry types. As an indeterminate variety, Wapsipinicon Peach continues to grow and produce throughout a long warm season, which is particularly advantageous in zone 9 where the long frost-free period extends production well into fall. Fruits cluster in modest-sized trusses and are best harvested when fully colored and just soft; they have a thinner skin than many modern cherry types, which is prized for fresh eating but means they are more susceptible to cracking after rain or irrigation following dry periods. Cage or stake firmly and prune suckers selectively to manage plant size and improve air circulation in humid conditions.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Wapsipinicon Peach Tomato heirloom variety history and regional origin notes
Carolyn Male, '100 Heirloom Tomatoes for the American Garden' (Storey Publishing, 1999) — Iowa heritage tomato documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Wapsipinicon Peach variety trials and flavor notes

common_name: Green Grape Tomato (Sweet Amber-Green Cherry Tomato that Ripens Green)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Green Grape is an indeterminate open-pollinated cherry tomato that retains green color at maturity, ripening to an amber-green with a slight golden blush when fully ripe. The key challenge for new growers is learning to identify ripe fruits by feel and slight color shift rather than waiting for red coloration that will never arrive. When fully ripe, Green Grape fruits yield slightly to gentle pressure and develop a translucent quality that distinguishes them from the opaque green of an immature tomato. In zone 9, transplant March–April; like all indeterminate types, Green Grape benefits from caging or staking to 5–6 feet and continues producing until heat stops fruit set in midsummer or until fall frost.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–85°F; transplant after last frost and cage or stake to 6 feet. Harvest when fruits yield to gentle pressure and show a slight translucent amber-green glow rather than the opaque dark green of unripe fruit. Green Grape Tomato is an open-pollinated cherry tomato variety that remains green at full maturity — a characteristic that surprises first-time growers accustomed to using color change as the primary ripeness cue. The variety was developed by Tim Peters of Peters Seed and Research in Oregon and introduced in the 1990s as part of a wave of interest in non-standard-colored tomatoes that offered novel flavors and visual interest compared to standard red types. Green Grape fruits are approximately 1 inch in diameter with a round to slightly oval shape and thin green skin that ripens to a yellow-green or amber-green with characteristic translucency at peak ripeness; experienced growers learn to identify this translucency as the reliable ripeness indicator. The flavor is notably sweet with a complex, slightly tropical undertone — a combination that many tasters find more interesting than the straightforward sweetness of yellow or red cherry types. The green color is not from chlorophyll retained past ripening but from specific chloroplast development patterns that persist through the normal ripening process; the fruit is fully ripe when the color reaches amber-green, not when it turns red. Fruits cluster in medium-sized trusses of 8–12 and tend to hold well on the vine for several days after reaching peak ripeness without cracking, which is an advantage for gardeners who can't harvest daily.
sources: Peters Seed and Research — Green Grape Tomato original variety development documentation
Seed Savers Exchange — Green Grape cherry tomato heirloom listing and grower notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Green Grape tomato variety description and ripeness identification tips

common_name: Jacob's Cattle Bean (Spotted New England Heritage Dry Bean with Red-Maroon Markings)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed to at least 60°F; Jacob's Cattle is an heirloom pole bean grown primarily as a dry bean, though young pods can also be eaten as snap beans before the seeds swell. The traditional pole-type plant requires a trellis or poles to 5–6 feet; a traditional Three Sisters support or fence works well. Seeds are distinctively patterned: creamy white background with deep maroon-red splashes and speckles that resemble the markings of a Dalmatian or spotted cattle — hence the name, which traces to a farmer named Jacob in New England who maintained a herd of spotted cattle and grew this bean. Let dry pods remain on the vine until fully papery before harvesting for storage.

directions: Direct sow after last frost, 1 inch deep, 4–6 inches apart in rows 18–24 inches apart; provide pole or trellis support to 6 feet and harvest dry pods when completely papery. Jacob's Cattle Bean is one of the best-documented New England heirloom dry beans, with a history tracing through Maine and surrounding states to the mid-nineteenth century and possibly earlier; the variety has been maintained continuously by generations of New England farm families and seed-savers, making it one of the most genetically stable and well-characterized heirloom dry beans in American seed-saving tradition. The beans' distinctive coloration — a white or ivory background with deep burgundy-maroon splashing and speckled markings — is one of the most visually striking patterns among heirloom beans and is the primary characteristic referenced in the variety's common names; 'Dalmatian bean' and 'Coach Dog bean' are historical synonyms that refer to the same spotted patterning. As a dry bean, Jacob's Cattle is prized for its meaty texture and mild, slightly earthy flavor that holds up well in long-cooked dishes — traditional New England baked beans, cassoulet-style preparations, and soup beans all showcase the variety's cooking qualities. The beans absorb surrounding flavors readily while maintaining structural integrity through several hours of cooking, a characteristic that distinguishes high-quality dry bean varieties from those that turn to mush. Young pods (harvested before seeds swell noticeably) are edible as snap beans, providing versatility across the growing season. For dry bean production, allow all pods to yellow and dry fully on the vine before harvest; shell by hand or place dry pods in a pillowcase and beat against a hard surface, then winnow chaff in a breeze.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Jacob's Cattle Bean heirloom history and Maine heritage documentation
William Woys Weaver, 'Heirloom Vegetable Gardening' (Henry Holt, 1997) — New England dry bean heritage varieties
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Dry Bean Variety Performance in Northern and Southern Garden Trials
